entities.name: dhvani
entities.cite: Each dhvani deposits a bija, a seed or trace, in the hearer's buddhi

entities.name: bija
entities.cite: Each dhvani deposits a bija, a seed or trace, in the hearer's buddhi

entities.name: buddhi
entities.cite: Each dhvani deposits a bija, a seed or trace, in the hearer's buddhi

entities.name: sphota
entities.cite: it is the occasion on which a cognition prepared by all the traces seizes the entire sphota

axioms.name: ahita
axioms.cite: Each dhvani deposits a bija, a seed or trace, in the hearer's buddhi

axioms.name: paripaka
axioms.cite: the sequence is a sowing, avrtta-paripaka, ripening through the repeated strokes

axioms.name: avadharyate
axioms.cite: together with the last sound, the whole word is ascertained at once

axioms.name: nimitta
axioms.cite: The final phoneme is not a final part completing a sum — it is the occasion on which a cognition prepared by all the traces seizes the entire sphota

axioms.name: yogyata
axioms.cite: what accumulates is a readiness in the cognizer, not an assembly of the object

axioms.name: samanya
axioms.cite: a unity over and above it

denials.name: samavayavata
denials.cite: what accumulates is a readiness in the cognizer, not an assembly of the object

claims.name: ahita
claims.cite: The seed having been sown by the sounds

claims.name: paripaka
claims.cite: in a cognition brought to ripeness by repetition

rejected_readings.label: mimamsa_phoneme_series
rejected_readings.rendering: The word-meaning is conveyed by the last phoneme working together with memory-traces of preceding phonemes to form a connected series of meanings.
rejected_readings.why: This is the Mimamsa rival account that the verse and commentary explicitly contrast with sphota doctrine. The commentary states: 'the argument between the two schools turns on whether the object of the final cognition is a mere phoneme-series remembered, or a unity over and above it'. Asserting sphota as a phoneme-series contradicts the axiom that sphota has samanya (unified whole), and contradicts the denial of samavayavata (composition from parts).

rejected_readings.label: piecemeal_construction
rejected_readings.rendering: The word is progressively built up by adding the contributions of each successive sound, until the final sound completes the whole.
rejected_readings.why: This misses the central distinction the verse makes: 'The final phoneme is not a final part completing a sum'. The commentary emphasizes: 'what accumulates is a readiness in the cognizer, not an assembly of the object'. The whole word (sphota) is ascertained as a unity when the final sound occurs, not constructed from accumulated parts. The reading denies that sphota is samanya (a non-composite unity).

rejected_readings.label: final_phoneme_as_part
rejected_readings.rendering: The final sound is the last phonetic part of the word, and its utterance completes the audible sequence in which the word's meaning is conveyed.
rejected_readings.why: Treats the final dhvani as a constitutive part (samavayavata) of the word, when the verse insists it is merely the occasion (nimitta) for grasping. The commentary is explicit: 'The final phoneme is not a final part completing a sum'. This reading fails to distinguish the final sound's role (occasion for ascertainment) from the constitution of the object (which remains whole and unassembled).

notes: The verse encodes the sphota mechanism for grasping a partless whole through temporal sound-sequence. Each sound deposits a trace (bija-ahita) in the hearer's awareness, ripening it through repetition (paripaka), and the final sound occasions the sudden ascertainment of the complete word as an undivided unity (sphota-samanya-avadharyate). Crucially, what accumulates is readiness-in-the-subject (yogyata-of-buddhi), not composition-of-the-object. This separates Bhartrhari's sphota theory from Mimamsa memory-plus-phoneme accounts.
